close_reason: By design. GCP access tokens are short-lived and expected to expire (unlike doppler/vault API keys). The 401→Unavailable mapping is intentional with documented recovery (reconstruct backend with fresh token). The inconsistency reflects genuinely different auth models, not a bug.
description: HTTP 401 has different semantic weight across backends. gcp-sm maps 401 → SecretError::Unavailable (rationale: expired token, caller should refresh and retry). doppler explicitly maps 401 → SecretError::Backend. vault falls through map_http_status where all non-5xx/non-429 → Backend. A caller writing generic retry logic against SecretStore cannot reliably distinguish 'retry after credential refresh' from 'permanent auth failure' because the same HTTP condition maps to different variants depending on which backend is underneath. Fix: decide on one policy (Unavailable for token-expiry-capable 401, Backend for credential-misconfiguration 401) and apply it consistently, or add a new SecretError::AuthFailed variant.
title: gcp-sm maps HTTP 401 to Unavailable; doppler/vault map it to Backend
